course_description:
Earth is a small planet, but with a big history. With its origin some 4.5 billion years ago, ERTH1000 will examine Earth's evolution, from both a geological and biological perspective. The course will explore concepts as diverse as the origin of the Solar System through to the evolution of life. It will touch on the big geological processes that have shaped our planet, covering topics including plate tectonics, volcanology, earthquakes and climate change. ERTH1000 is recommended for all students in geology, ecology, biology, marine, environmental science and archaeology, and is also appropriate for anyone who wants to know more about the world they live on. Includes a one-day field trip (additional fee may be payable).

This course will provide theﾠperfect entry level overview of modern Earth Science.

The course is compulsory for BSc students who intend to major in Earth Sciences, Coastal and Ocean Sciences, Archaeological Sciences and the Bachelors of Environmental Management (Honours). However, the course is appropriate for students from other fields, including the arts and social sciences, who simply want to know more about the world around them. The course spans one semester and is worth 2 units.

A full day field trip will be run with two groups and students will attend on EITHER Saturday 18th April OR Sunday 19th April. Field trips have an additional cost of around $40 (TBC). All students enrolled in ERTH1000 are expected to attend the field trip however if you are unable to attend and have a good reason please contact the course co-ordinator in advance.ﾠ

To get an idea of what the course is like click on the link ﾠ here

course_aims: On completing this course students will gain an introduction to fundamental principles and processes in Earth Sciences, such as:

learning_outcomes.description: Identify earth materials including basic minerals, different rock types and important fossil groups.
Explain how we know the structure and character of the Earths core, mantle, and crust and their general composition.
Articulate how the phenomenon of plate tectonics leads to the evolution of the Earth's surface, climate, biosphere, hydrosphere and cryosphere

Evaluate how the fundamental earth processes and materials relate to human impacts on the environment, with an emphasis on the importance of Earth Sciences in the understanding and management of the natural environment.

Apply your knowledge of rock types, geological time, tectonic processes and fossil to create a geological history of South East Queensland. Understand how this links to the evolution of the earth through time.

assessment_details.task_description: All practical exercises will be assessable. The total practical component of ERTH1000 is weighted at 20%. Completed practical documents are to be submitted for assessment via Inspera the week following the Practical, as outlined by the due dates. Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Online submission via Inspera only by the due date and time. Refer to Blackboard for the submission details.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
If you are granted an approved extension you will need to contact the Course coordinator to discuss the alternate arrangement.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
You are required to submit assessable items on time. If you fail to meet the submission deadline for any assessment item, then 10% of the maximum possible mark for the assessment item (the assessment ‘marked from’ value) will be deducted as a late penalty for every day (or part day) late after the due date.
For example, if you submit your assignment 1 hour late, you will be penalised 10%; if your assignment is 24.5 hours late, you will be penalised 20% (because it is late by one 24-hour period plus part of another 24-hour period).

assessment_details.title: Field trip notes and report
assessment_details.task_description: You will submit a field report and your notes from fieldwork to two sites in Northern New South Wales. This will develop your skills in recognising rocks, understanding the processes and environments in which they form. The field trip is a compulsory component of the course. For students who miss the field trip, you may only be eligible for alternative arrangement if exceptional circumstances arise that prevent you from attending the fieldtrip. Please contact your course coordinator with supporting documentation of the exceptional circumstances that prevented you from attending for consideration of an alternate arrangement. Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. We will work through approaches to using AI tools for your report during class time. Read full task description Hide full task description

learning_resources.library_resources: Library resources are available on the UQ Library website .

learning_activities.topic: Weekly Lectures In person lectures, recordings are made and will be available on Blackboard Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05

Earth Science Laboratory Practicals Practicals are three hours and will take place most weeks but see timetable. In Week 6 we will undertake a short field trip to prepare the class with the skills for the main field trip in week 7. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

Field trip The field trip is a compulsory component of the course. Weekend field trip to two sites in Northern NSW to examine local rock formations and produce a report. Students need only attend ONE of the days on this weekend which will happen at the end of week 7 on the 18th OR the 19th of April. Learning outcomes: L01, L04, L05

policies_and_procedures: University policies and procedures apply to all aspects of student life. As a UQ student, you must comply with University-wide and program-specific requirements, including the: Student Code of Conduct Policy Student Integrity and Misconduct Policy and Procedure Assessment Procedure Examinations Procedure Reasonable Adjustments for Students Policy and Procedure AI for Assessment Guide Recording of Teaching Policy and Procedure Learn more about UQ policies on my.UQ and the Policy and Procedure Library .
